seoTitle: TDK Technical Support | Passive Component Application Guides
seoDescription: Technical support resources for TDK capacitors and inductors including application guides, selection tools, FAQs, and engineering documentation.

faqs.question: What technical support does LiTong provide for TDK products?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive technical support for TDK passive components through our experienced Field Application Engineering (FAE) team. Our support services include component selection guidance based on your specific application requirements, SPICE model support for circuit simulation, and design review services to optimize your designs. We offer application-specific recommendations for ceramic capacitors, aluminum electrolytic capacitors, film capacitors, and inductors. Our FAEs can assist with derating guidelines, thermal analysis, and reliability predictions. We also provide sample kits for evaluation, reference designs, and access to TDK's extensive technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, and white papers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team through the support portal or by email for personalized technical assistance with your TDK component needs.

faqs.question: How do I access TDK datasheets and technical documentation?
faqs.answer: TDK datasheets and technical documentation are available through multiple channels. All product pages on our website include links to official TDK datasheets in PDF format. Technical articles and application notes are available in our support section, covering topics such as capacitor selection, inductor design, EMI filtering, and reliability considerations. For specialized documentation not available online, our FAE team can request materials directly from TDK's technical support organization. We also maintain a library of reference designs and evaluation board documentation for common applications. Contact our support team if you need specific documentation for your design project.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our product pages for datasheets, or visit the support section for application notes and technical articles.

faqs.question: Can LiTong provide samples of TDK components?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong Electronics provides sample kits of TDK components for evaluation and prototyping. Sample requests can be submitted through our website or by contacting your local sales representative. We stock samples of popular TDK series including ceramic capacitors (C series, CGA series), aluminum electrolytic capacitors (B4350x, B4370x series), film capacitors (B3277x, B3265x series), and power inductors (SLF series, ACM series). For high-volume or specialized samples, we can coordinate with TDK's factory to provide the specific parts you need. Sample kits typically ship within 1-2 business days for stocked items.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit sample requests through our online form or contact sales for specialized sample requirements.

faqs.question: Does LiTong offer design review services for TDK-based designs?
faqs.answer: Yes, our FAE team offers design review services for customers implementing TDK components in their designs. The design review process includes schematic review to verify component selection and ratings, PCB layout recommendations for optimal performance and thermal management, and BOM analysis to identify potential issues or optimization opportunities. We can review designs for automotive, industrial, consumer, and renewable energy applications. For complex designs, we can arrange conference calls or meetings with our FAEs and TDK's application engineers. Design reviews are typically completed within 3-5 business days depending on complexity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit your design files through our support portal to request a design review with our FAE team.

faqs.question: What is the typical response time for technical support inquiries?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics strives to provide prompt technical support for all customer inquiries. Standard technical questions submitted through our support portal typically receive a response within 24 business hours. Urgent inquiries for production-critical issues are prioritized and typically receive a response within 4 hours during business hours. For complex technical questions requiring detailed analysis or consultation with TDK's factory experts, response times may be 2-3 business days. Our FAEs are available by phone during business hours (8:00 AM - 6:00 PM local time) for immediate assistance with critical issues. For customers with dedicated account support, direct FAE contact information is provided for fastest response.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use the support portal for standard inquiries; contact your FAE directly or call for urgent production issues.

faqs.question: Can LiTong provide SPICE models for TDK components?
faqs.answer: Yes, SPICE models are available for many TDK components to support circuit simulation and design validation. SPICE models for ceramic capacitors include frequency-dependent characteristics such as ESR and ESL. Inductor models include saturation characteristics and core loss parameters. We can provide models in various formats including PSPICE, LTspice, and generic SPICE. For components where official TDK models are not available, our FAE team can assist with creating simplified models based on datasheet parameters or recommend equivalent models from similar parts. Contact our technical support with your specific simulation needs and preferred simulator platform.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request SPICE models through technical support, specifying your simulator platform and application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate component lifetime for my application?
faqs.answer: Component lifetime calculation depends on the specific component type and operating conditions. For aluminum electrolytic capacitors, lifetime follows the Arrhenius equation - approximately doubling for every 10C decrease in temperature. Voltage derating also extends lifetime, typically doubling for every 20% reduction in operating voltage. Our FAE team can provide detailed lifetime calculations based on your specific voltage, temperature, and ripple current conditions. For film capacitors, lifetime follows an inverse power law with voltage stress. Ceramic capacitors generally have very long lifetimes when operated within ratings. We provide lifetime calculation tools and can perform custom analysis for critical applications. Contact our FAE team with your operating conditions for detailed reliability predictions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Provide your operating conditions (voltage, temperature, ripple current) to our FAE team for detailed lifetime calculations.

faqs.question: What training resources are available for TDK products?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics offers various training resources for engineers working with TDK components. We conduct periodic technical seminars and webinars covering topics such as capacitor selection, inductor design, EMI filtering, and power supply design. Recordings of past webinars are available in our support section. We can arrange on-site training sessions for customers with specific needs, covering TDK product portfolios and application best practices. Our FAE team also presents at industry conferences and trade shows. For self-paced learning, we provide application notes, white papers, and technical articles covering fundamental concepts and advanced design techniques. Contact our marketing team to schedule training for your engineering team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Check our events page for upcoming webinars, or contact us to schedule custom training for your team.

articles.title: MLCC Selection Guide for Power Applications
articles.shortDescription: Comprehensive guide for selecting multilayer ceramic capacitors for power supply and decoupling applications.
articles.content:
## Introduction to MLCC Selection

Multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) are essential components in modern electronic designs, offering high capacitance density, low ESR, and excellent high-frequency performance. This guide covers key considerations for selecting MLCCs in power applications.

## Understanding Dielectric Types

### Class I Dielectrics (C0G/NP0)
Class I dielectrics offer the highest stability with near-zero temperature coefficient (0 plus or minus 30 ppm/C). These capacitors are ideal for:
- Timing circuits
- Oscillator circuits
- Filter applications requiring precise capacitance
- High-Q RF circuits

Characteristics:
- Excellent capacitance stability over temperature
- Minimal capacitance loss with DC bias
- Low capacitance density (higher cost per uF)
- No aging effects

### Class II Dielectrics (X7R, X5R)
Class II dielectrics provide higher capacitance density at lower cost, making them suitable for:
- Decoupling and bypass applications
- Bulk capacitance for power supplies
- General-purpose filtering

Characteristics:
- X7R: plus or minus 15% capacitance variation (-55C to +125C)
- X5R: plus or minus 15% capacitance variation (-55C to +85C)
- Significant DC bias effects
- Aging effects (typically 2-5% per decade hour)

## DC Bias Effects

DC bias significantly affects Class II ceramic capacitors. When DC voltage is applied, the dielectric material experiences reduced permittivity, causing capacitance to decrease. This effect is more pronounced in smaller package sizes.

### Mitigation Strategies
1. Use larger package sizes for better DC bias characteristics
2. Select higher nominal capacitance values
3. Consider C0G dielectric for critical applications
4. Account for capacitance reduction in design calculations

## Voltage Derating

TDK recommends operating MLCCs at 70-80% of rated voltage for optimal reliability and lifetime. Benefits of voltage derating include:
- Extended operational life
- Reduced leakage current
- Improved reliability under surge conditions
- Margin for transient overvoltages

## Package Size Selection

Select package size based on:
- Required capacitance and voltage rating
- PCB space constraints
- Manufacturing process capabilities
- Thermal considerations

Common sizes:
- 0402, 0603: Consumer electronics, high-density designs
- 0805, 1206: General-purpose industrial applications
- 1210 and larger: High-voltage, high-capacitance requirements

## Application-Specific Recommendations

### DC-DC Converter Input/Output
- Use X7R dielectric for temperature stability
- Select package size based on ripple current requirements
- Parallel multiple capacitors for high-current applications
- Include smaller capacitors for high-frequency decoupling

### Automotive Applications
- Select AEC-Q200 qualified components (CGA series)
- Use X8R dielectric for high-temperature locations
- Apply additional voltage derating for reliability
- Verify vibration resistance requirements

## Conclusion

Proper MLCC selection requires understanding dielectric characteristics, DC bias effects, and application requirements. TDK's extensive MLCC portfolio offers solutions for virtually every application need. Contact our FAE team for application-specific recommendations.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: In our experience supporting power supply designs, the most common issue with MLCCs is insufficient consideration of DC bias effects. Designers often select capacitors based on nominal capacitance without accounting for the significant reduction under DC bias. For example, a 10uF X5R capacitor in 0603 package may provide only 3-4uF effective capacitance at 50% rated voltage. We always recommend using TDK's DC bias curves and applying at least 50% margin in capacitance calculations. For critical applications, measuring actual capacitance under operating conditions is the best practice.
keyTakeaways: Always account for DC bias effects in capacitance calculations
Use larger packages or higher nominal values for margin
Verify temperature characteristics match application requirements

steps: Define capacitance, voltage, and temperature requirements
Select dielectric type based on stability needs
Choose package size considering DC bias effects
Verify ripple current and thermal requirements
decisionPoints: Class I vs Class II dielectric selection
Package size vs capacitance density trade-off

faeInsights.insightLogic: Based on extensive field experience and customer interactions, our FAE team has developed these insights to help engineers optimize their designs. The recommendations are derived from real-world applications and best practices.

faqs.question: How much does DC bias affect capacitance?
faqs.answer: DC bias effects vary significantly based on dielectric type, package size, and voltage. For X5R/X7R capacitors in small packages (0402, 0603), capacitance can drop 60-80% at 50% rated voltage. Larger packages (1206, 1210) typically show 30-50% reduction. C0G dielectric is virtually unaffected by DC bias. TDK provides DC bias characteristic curves in datasheets. For critical applications, designers should measure actual capacitance under operating conditions or use larger packages to minimize the effect.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TDK's DC bias curves in datasheets; apply 50-100% capacitance margin for Class II dielectrics.

faqs.question: What is the difference between X7R and X5R dielectrics?
faqs.answer: X7R and X5R are both Class II ceramic dielectrics with different temperature ranges. X7R is rated for -55C to +125C with plus or minus 15% capacitance variation. X5R is rated for -55C to +85C with the same plus or minus 15% variation. X7R is preferred for industrial and automotive applications with wide temperature requirements. X5R is suitable for consumer electronics with moderate temperature ranges and offers slightly lower cost. Both exhibit similar DC bias characteristics and aging effects. For high-temperature applications above 125C, X8R dielectric (-55C to +150C) is available.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select X7R for industrial/automotive; X5R for cost-sensitive consumer applications; X8R for high temperature.

faqs.question: How do I calculate ripple current for MLCCs?
faqs.answer: Ripple current in MLCCs causes self-heating due to ESR. The temperature rise can be calculated as: delta T = I squared times ESR times Rth, where I is RMS ripple current, ESR is equivalent series resistance at the ripple frequency, and Rth is thermal resistance. TDK specifies maximum ripple current ratings based on a 20C temperature rise. For reliable operation, keep actual temperature rise below this limit. At higher frequencies, ESR decreases, allowing higher ripple current capability. For high-ripple applications, use multiple capacitors in parallel to distribute current and thermal load.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate temperature rise and ensure it stays within ratings; parallel capacitors for high-ripple applications.

faqs.question: What causes MLCCs to fail?
faqs.answer: MLCC failure modes include mechanical cracking from thermal shock or board flexure, insulation resistance degradation under high temperature and voltage, and silver migration in the electrodes. Mechanical cracking is the most common failure, caused by excessive solder stress, board bending, or thermal shock during soldering. Class II capacitors can also fail due to capacitance drift outside specification from aging or DC bias effects. Proper PCB design, appropriate soldering profiles, and voltage derating minimize failure risk. TDK's automotive-grade (CGA) capacitors undergo additional testing for enhanced reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow proper PCB design guidelines and soldering profiles; apply voltage derating for reliability.

faqs.question: Can MLCCs be used in series for higher voltage?
faqs.answer: Yes, MLCCs can be connected in series to achieve higher voltage ratings, but this requires careful consideration. When connecting in series, voltage balancing is required as capacitor characteristics vary. Use identical part numbers and add parallel balancing resistors (typically 100k ohm to 1M ohm) to ensure equal voltage distribution. For two 50V capacitors in series, the combination can theoretically handle 100V, but practical designs should limit to 80V (80% of theoretical) to account for imbalance. Series connection also reduces effective capacitance by half. Consider using a single higher voltage rated capacitor when available.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use voltage balancing resistors; derate the series combination; consider single higher voltage capacitor.

customerCases.challenge: Experienced capacitor failures in high-temperature DC-DC converters due to insufficient DC bias consideration
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with larger package sizes and applied proper DC bias derating based on TDK application guide
customerCases.feedback: Following the MLCC selection guide eliminated our field failures. The DC bias curves were particularly helpful for proper sizing.

articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting multilayer ceramic capacitors for power supply and decoupling applications, covering dielectric types, DC bias effects, and application recommendations.

articles.title: Aluminum Electrolytic Capacitor Lifetime Prediction
articles.shortDescription: Understanding lifetime factors and calculation methods for aluminum electrolytic capacitors in power applications.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Capacitor Lifetime

Aluminum electrolytic capacitors are essential components in power supplies, motor drives, and energy storage applications. Understanding their lifetime characteristics is crucial for designing reliable systems.

## Primary Failure Mechanism

The primary wear-out mechanism in aluminum electrolytic capacitors is electrolyte evaporation through the seal. As electrolyte is lost over time, capacitance decreases gradually and ESR increases.

## Temperature Effects

Temperature has the most significant impact on capacitor lifetime. The relationship follows the Arrhenius equation where lifetime approximately doubles for every 10C decrease in temperature.

## Voltage Effects

Operating voltage also affects lifetime. Operating at 80% of rated voltage typically doubles the expected lifetime compared to full rated voltage operation.

## Ripple Current Effects

Ripple current causes internal heating due to I squared R losses in the ESR. This self-heating adds to ambient temperature, reducing lifetime.

## Design Recommendations

Apply voltage derating and thermal management to maximize capacitor lifetime. TDK provides detailed lifetime calculation tools.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: In industrial power supply applications, we frequently see designs that don't account for the combined effects of temperature and voltage on capacitor lifetime. We recommend designing for worst-case conditions including maximum ambient temperature and ripple current. For 24/7 industrial applications, we typically target 100,000+ hour lifetime.
keyTakeaways: Temperature has the greatest impact on lifetime
Voltage derating of 20% typically doubles lifetime
faeInsights.insightLogic: Based on extensive field experience and customer interactions, our FAE team has developed these insights to help engineers optimize their designs. The recommendations are derived from real-world applications and best practices.

faqs.question: What is the typical end-of-life criteria for aluminum electrolytic capacitors?
faqs.answer: End-of-life is typically defined as when capacitance drops below 80% of initial value OR when ESR increases above 200% of initial value. These criteria ensure the capacitor can still perform its basic function.
faqs.decisionGuide: Monitor capacitance and ESR during maintenance; plan replacement before reaching end-of-life criteria.

faqs.question: How does frequency affect ripple current capability?
faqs.answer: ESR of aluminum electrolytic capacitors decreases with increasing frequency, allowing higher ripple current capability at higher frequencies. At 10kHz-20kHz, ESR is typically 30-50% lower than at 100Hz.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use frequency-specific ESR data for accurate ripple current calculations.

faqs.question: Can capacitor lifetime be extended by reducing voltage?
faqs.answer: Yes, voltage derating significantly extends capacitor lifetime. Operating at 80% of rated voltage typically doubles lifetime. Operating at 70% can provide 3-4x lifetime extension.
faqs.decisionGuide: Apply 20-30% voltage derating for significant lifetime improvement.

faqs.question: What is the shelf life of aluminum electrolytic capacitors?
faqs.answer: TDK recommends a maximum storage period of 3 years at room temperature. During storage, the electrolyte can slowly evaporate and the oxide layer may degrade slightly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use capacitors within 3 years of manufacture; perform reformation if stored longer.

faqs.question: How do I measure capacitor health in the field?
faqs.answer: Field measurement involves measuring capacitance and ESR using an LCR meter. Compare measured values to initial specifications: capacitance should be within plus or minus 20% of nominal, ESR should not exceed datasheet maximum.
faqs.decisionGuide: Measure capacitance and ESR during maintenance; track trends over time.

customerCases.challenge: Capacitor failures in motor drives operating in high-temperature environments
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with improved thermal management and applied 30% voltage derating
customerCases.feedback: Went from 3-year failures to expected 15+ year lifetime

articles.summary: Understanding lifetime factors and calculation methods for aluminum electrolytic capacitors in power applications.

articles.title: Film Capacitor Applications in Power Electronics
articles.shortDescription: Application guide for film capacitors in inverters, motor drives, and power conversion systems.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Film Capacitors

Film capacitors are essential components in high-performance power electronics applications. Their unique characteristics including self-healing properties and excellent frequency response make them ideal for demanding applications.

## Film Capacitor Technology

Film capacitors consist of thin plastic film dielectric with metal electrodes. Polypropylene film provides the lowest losses and best frequency characteristics.

## DC Link Applications

DC link capacitors serve as energy storage between rectifier and inverter stages. Key requirements include high capacitance, low ESR, and high ripple current capability.

## Snubber Applications

Snubber capacitors protect power semiconductors from voltage transients. Design considerations include low ESL and high dV/dt capability.

## Self-Healing Properties

Metallized film capacitors exhibit unique self-healing behavior where localized dielectric breakdowns are automatically cleared without catastrophic failure.

## Conclusion

Film capacitors provide superior performance for high-power electronics applications with their self-healing properties and long lifetime.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: In renewable energy applications, we frequently see designers underestimating the importance of ESL in DC link capacitors. With modern SiC MOSFETs switching at high speeds, even small ESL can cause significant voltage overshoot.
keyTakeaways: ESL is critical for high-frequency applications
faeInsights.insightLogic: Based on extensive field experience and customer interactions, our FAE team has developed these insights to help engineers optimize their designs. The recommendations are derived from real-world applications and best practices.

faqs.question: When should I choose film capacitors over aluminum electrolytic?
faqs.answer: Choose film capacitors when high ripple current, long lifetime, or high-frequency switching is needed. Choose aluminum electrolytic for highest capacitance density or cost-sensitive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select film for high-reliability applications; electrolytic for cost-sensitive needs.

faqs.question: What is the self-healing process?
faqs.answer: When dielectric breakdown occurs, a small arc vaporizes the thin metallization around the fault site, isolating the defect. The capacitor continues operating with slightly reduced capacitance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use metallized film capacitors for safety-critical applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate required DC link capacitance?
faqs.answer: DC link capacitance is calculated based on allowable voltage ripple. The formula is C equals I ripple divided by (8 times f switching times V ripple).
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on ripple requirements; verify thermal capability.

faqs.question: What is dV/dt rating?
faqs.answer: dV/dt specifies the maximum rate of voltage change a capacitor can withstand. Modern SiC devices create very high dV/dt which can stress capacitors.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify dV/dt rating exceeds application requirements.

faqs.question: Can film capacitors be connected in parallel?
faqs.answer: Yes, film capacitors can be paralleled to increase capacitance and current handling. Current sharing is naturally balanced with symmetrical layout.
faqs.decisionGuide: Parallel capacitors for increased capacitance; use symmetrical layout.

customerCases.challenge: Needed reliable DC link capacitors for 1500V inverters with 25-year lifetime
customerCases.solution: TDK B3277x series film capacitors with voltage derating
customerCases.feedback: Eliminated DC link reliability concerns for remote installations

articles.summary: Application guide for film capacitors in inverters and power conversion systems.

articles.title: Power Inductor Selection for DC-DC Converters
articles.shortDescription: Comprehensive guide for selecting power inductors including saturation current and thermal considerations.
articles.content:
## Introduction to Power Inductor Selection

Power inductors are critical components in DC-DC converters. Proper selection requires understanding inductance requirements, current ratings, and core materials.

## Inductance Calculation

The required inductance is calculated based on allowable ripple current. The formula considers input voltage, output voltage, switching frequency, and desired ripple.

## Current Ratings

Rated current is based on thermal limitations. Saturation current is the current at which inductance drops significantly. Both must be considered.

## Core Material Selection

Ferrite cores offer low losses at high frequencies. Metal composite cores provide soft saturation and higher saturation flux density.

## Shielded vs Unshielded

Shielded inductors minimize EMI radiation. Unshielded inductors are smaller and lower cost but produce more EMI.

## Conclusion

Proper power inductor selection requires balancing multiple parameters. TDK's portfolio offers solutions for every application.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: The most common mistake we see in inductor selection is focusing only on inductance value while ignoring current ratings. Designers calculate they need 10uH and select any 10uH inductor without verifying saturation current. In a buck converter with 2A output and 0.6A ripple, the peak current is 2.3A. If the selected inductor has 2.0A saturation current, it will saturate during normal operation, causing increased ripple, losses, and potential instability. We always recommend calculating both average and peak currents, then selecting an inductor with at least 30% margin on saturation current. Also, remember that saturation current decreases at high temperatures.
keyTakeaways: Calculate both average and peak currents
Verify SRF is above switching frequency
faeInsights.insightLogic: Based on extensive field experience and customer interactions, our FAE team has developed these insights to help engineers optimize their designs. The recommendations are derived from real-world applications and best practices.

faqs.question: What is inductor saturation?
faqs.answer: Saturation occurs when the core material reaches maximum flux density and inductance drops dramatically. This causes increased ripple current and potential converter instability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for peak current 30% below saturation rating.

faqs.question: How do ferrite and metal composite compare?
faqs.answer: Ferrite offers low losses at high frequencies with sharp saturation. Metal composite provides soft saturation and higher flux density but higher losses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select ferrite for high frequency; metal composite for high current.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rated and saturation current?
faqs.answer: Rated current is thermal limitation. Saturation current is where inductance drops. In converters, saturation current is usually the limiting factor.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for both limits; saturation is typically limiting.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect inductors?
faqs.answer: DCR increases with temperature. Saturation current decreases at high temperatures. Core losses may change with temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Account for temperature effects on DCR and saturation.

faqs.question: Can inductors be paralleled?
faqs.answer: Yes, but current sharing depends on inductance matching. Practical designs should use only 1.6-1.8x single inductor rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use single higher-current inductor when possible.

customerCases.challenge: Inductor saturation causing instability in high-temperature equipment
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with metal composite inductors having better temperature characteristics
customerCases.feedback: Eliminated field issues with soft saturation characteristic

articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting power inductors including saturation current and thermal considerations.
